data.title: Mississippi to hold hearing on xAI data center amid pollution lawsuit threat: report
data.description:
On Tuesday, Mississippi regulators will host a public hearing over plans by xAI (X.AI) to develop a gas-fired power plant to power its Colossus II data center, Reuters reported.

The AI startup faces a lawsuit threat by the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People, or NAACP, accusing the company of violating federal law by installing and operating gas turbines without air permits, the report added.

NAACP, acting on behalf of the largely African American local community living near the existing Colossus I facility in Memphis, Tennessee, noted that xAI started illegally installing and then operating 27 gas turbines at a site in Southaven, Mississippi, to power Colossus II, sited across the state line in Memphis, the report noted.

The organization said that this was being done without properly receiving the required preconstruction or operating air permits under the Clean Air Act.

xAI did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

"Pollution from these turbines is worsening and will continue to worsen the already poor air quality in Southaven, Mississippi and the Memphis metropolitan area," said the NAACP in a letter declaring its intent to sue xAI, according to the report.

The NAACP noted that the turbines have the potential to release a significant amount of smog-forming nitrogen oxides (NOx), above the Clean Air Act’s “major source” threshold, and other pollutants, including fine particulate matter and carcinogenic formaldehyde, harming largely African American local communities, the report added.

The Clean Air Act requires plaintiffs to indicate their intent to sue 60 days in advance.

Elon Musk's xAI has been building its Colossus supercomputer, which is being used to train the company's AI chatbot Grok in Memphis and expanding to Southaven. It is currently in phase 2 and is looking to start a third phase soon, the report noted.

Following the Southern Environmental Law Center, or SELC, which is representing the NAACP in this new lawsuit threat, filing an intent to sue xAI in 2024 for installing 35 non-permitted turbines at its Colossus 1 site, the firm removed 20 turbines and received permits for the remaining 15, the report added.

The Mississippi Department of Environmental Quality, or DEQ, hearing is the only chance for the public to discuss the project, which SELC has noted will be the largest new source of pollution in the area around Memphis in years, the report added.

The DEQ said xAI has filed permit applications for 41 permanent turbines at the site and will run some temporary turbines while the applications are being reviewed, the report noted.

data.title: DTE Energy nears deal to serve second major Michigan data center, CEO says
data.description:
DTE Energy (DTE) shares are little changed in Tuesday's trading after reporting better-than-expected Q4 adjusted earnings and saying it expects to finalize a new data center agreement in Michigan in the coming weeks.

DTE Energy (DTE) is making "great progress" toward reaching an agreement with an undisclosed data center customer and is expecting to finalize a deal "in the coming weeks," President and CEO Joi Harris said on the company's earnings conference call.

The utility said it negotiated its first hyperscale data center contracts in Q4 to provide 1.4 GW of load to power Oracle's new data center in Saline Township, Michigan, which it said would create $300M in annual benefits for its existing customers, and has another 3 GW of load in advanced discussions that could be finalized in the coming weeks.

The CEO also said the company's data center contracts would be structured so that existing customers would not be burdened with the cost of powering them.

DTE (DTE) raised its five-year spending plan by 20% to $36.5B, up from the $30B it expected in its 2025-29 plan, intended primarily to serve data center customers.

The company reported Q4 adjusted earnings of $1.65/share, above the $1.52/share FactSet consensus estimate; adjusted profit from its electric segment rose 8.2% Y/Y to $211M, and its gas segment profit jumped 16.3% to $121M.

data.title: Fed's Barr wants more evidence on receding inflation before cutting rates further
data.description:
The risk of persistent inflation remaining above the central bank's 2% goal "is significant, which means we need to remain vigilant," Federal Reserve Governor Michael Barr said in a speech on Tuesday.

And while last week's employment report showed evidence that the labor market is stabilizing, "job creation has been near zero over the course of last year, as has labor force growth," he noted. The the low-hire, low-fire environment is in a "tentative balance in labor supply and demand," which could make the labor market "especially vulnerable to negative shocks," Barr said in a speech at the New York Association for Business Economics.

With the lack of progress in inflation slowing to 2% and the lower rate of job creation, the central banker sees no reason to rush to cut the federal funds rate further.

"I would like to see evidence that goods price inflation is sustainably retreating before considering reducing the policy rate further, provided labor market conditions remain stable," he said. "Based on current conditions and the data in hand, it will likely be appropriate to hold rates steady for some time as we assess incoming data, the evolving outlook, and the balance of risks."

His speech, though, focused on how artificial intelligence may impact the labor market, and with it, monetary policy. To sum it up, so far, Barr doesn't see a case to lower policy rates due to expected increases in productivity.

"In the event that GenAI results in a long-lasting boost to productivity growth, wages, and economic activity could grow more than would otherwise be the case without putting upward pressure on inflation," he said. "At the same time, demand for capital would rise because of the strong business investment required to take advantage of the technology, putting upward pressures on interest rates, and household savings could fall due to expectations of stronger real wage growth and thus higher lifetime earnings, also putting upward pressure on interest rates." 

The implication is that the neutral rate — or the point at which interest rates neither stimulate nor depress the economy — could rise as a result of higher productivity. Also, in the short term, investment in AI could be inflationary. "For all of these reasons, I expect that the AI boom is unlikely to be a reason for lowering policy rates," Barr said.

data.title: Paramount shares rise after Warner Bros. agrees to discussions for a possible deal
data.description:
Shares of Paramount Skydance (PSKY) are up in afternoon trading on Tuesday after Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) agreed to have discussions with the company and hash out terms for a possible merger between the two, following at least ten failed offers for the HBO parent.

PSKY surged nearly 10% to a session high of $11.33; the stock is currently up more than 6%. Paramount's strong ties to the current Trump administration and Warner Bros.' announcement to return to the table for negotiations are positively affecting the shares.

Over 7.17M shares have changed hands by 1:15 pm ET, closer to its three-month average trading volume of 8.77M.

Albeit Paramount's latest amended offer was not enough for WBD CEO David Zaslav and was rejected, it caught his attention. 

David Ellison's latest offer notably included a “ticking fee” of $650M payable each quarter after 2026 to WBD shareholders if the regulatory approvals are delayed. Among other things, Ellison also agreed to fund the $2.8B termination fee to Netflix and backstop a debt exchange offer that would relieve Warner Bros. of $1.5B in financing costs.

Warner Bros., in a bid to provide maximum value to its shareholders, made use of the waiver in its current arrangement with Netflix to have discussions with Paramount and laid down an ultimatum of seven days for a "best and final offer." 

WBD has also clearly indicated that it wants more than $31 per share for the entire company and that Netflix retains the right to match Paramount's offer.

If Paramount offers $33 per share, the PSKY-WBD deal is valued at nearly $116B, and at nearly $122B at $35 per share, inclusive of debt.

Warner agreeing to discussions with Paramount also comes amid the exit of Abigail Slater, the head of the antitrust division at the Justice Department, who determines the outcome of cases involving mergers and acquisitions.

Media reports suggested that Slater undermined pending cases due to differences of opinion and had disobeyed leadership.

Paramount shares are down nearly 30% so far this year, while Netflix is down 20%; WBD stock and the S&P 500 are down only marginally for the same period.

data.title: Is Kontoor Brands' new severance package a sign of more exec departures? -- analyst
data.description:
On February 12, Kontoor Brands (KTB) adopted a new Executive Severance Plan ((ESP)), releasing the details in a filing with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission on Friday. While the new severance package would have been considered a non-event before the long holiday weekend, BNP Paribas analyst Laurent Vasilescu suggests it could signal another round of executive departures.

Rather than a revision to a prior severance plan, the ESP is a new guideline for exiting executives and comes in three tiers that apply adjusted severance for chief executives (Tier One), executive vice presidents (Tier Two), and senior vice presidents and others (Tier Three). This comes on the heels of high-level departures over the past two years, including Carrie Ask, who stepped down as Helly Hansen’s CEO last summer.

“Are we going to see additional executive departures?” Vasilescu asks. “If so, we believe this would be troublesome, considering the company made a transformative acquisition with Helly Hansen less than a year ago and Lee’s topline continues to decline mid-single digits/high-single digits.”

BNP Paribas gives Kontoor Brands an Underperform rating, reflecting shrinking sales in its core business and overpayment for the stagnant Helly Hansen business.

data.title: SunCoke anticipates $230M–$250M adjusted EBITDA in 2026 following Phoenix Global integration and contract extensions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SunCoke Energy, Inc. (SXC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Katherine Gates, CEO, announced Mark Marinko’s retirement and Shantanu Agrawal’s appointment as CFO, highlighting continuity in financial discipline and operational focus. Gates stated, "I want to share a few highlights from 2025 before I turn it over to Mark to review the results in detail. First, I want to recognize another year with remarkable safety performance. SunCoke, excluding Phoenix, ended the year with a total recordable incident rate of 0.55."
  • Gates reported consolidated adjusted EBITDA of $219.2 million for 2025, impacted by the addition of Phoenix Global for five months and lower terminals handling volumes. She added, "The Domestic Coke segment was impacted by the change in mix of contract and spot coke sales, coupled with lower economics on the Granite City contract extension and the breach of contract by Algoma."
  • SunCoke extended its Granite City contract with U.S. Steel through December 2026 and Haverhill II with Cleveland-Cliffs through December 2028, maintaining similar economics to prior agreements. The KRT coal handling agreement began in Q2 2025, with full-year benefit expected in 2026.
  • The Phoenix acquisition and its integration were highlighted, with $41 million returned to shareholders via quarterly dividends in 2025 and plans to continue dividends in 2026.
  • Mark Marinko, CFO, stated, "The fourth quarter net loss attributable to SunCoke was $1 per share, down $1.28 versus the fourth quarter of 2024, primarily driven by onetime items totaling $0.85 per share net of tax, including a noncash asset impairment charge, primarily due to the closure of Haverhill I."
  • Marinko detailed that consolidated adjusted EBITDA for Q4 2025 was $56.7 million. Operating cash flow was $109.1 million for 2025, with a $30 million impact from the Algoma contract breach.

Outlook

  • SunCoke expects consolidated adjusted EBITDA between $230 million and $250 million in 2026. The Domestic Coke segment is projected to deliver adjusted EBITDA between $162 million and $168 million, with sales of approximately 3.4 million tons. Industrial Services is expected to contribute $90 million to $100 million in adjusted EBITDA, benefiting from a full year of Phoenix Global and improved terminal market conditions.
  • Corporate expenses are forecasted to increase by $5 million to $9 million due to normalized employee bonuses and Phoenix IT costs.
  • Operating cash flow for 2026 is anticipated between $230 million and $250 million, with free cash flow of $140 million to $150 million. CapEx is expected to range between $90 million and $100 million.
  • The company plans continued deleveraging with a target year-end gross leverage of 2.45x and will maintain its quarterly dividend policy.

Financial Results

  • Full year net loss attributable to SunCoke was $0.52 per share, impacted by $0.97 per share in onetime items, including a noncash asset impairment charge related to the closure of Haverhill I and Phoenix-related costs.
  • Domestic Coke full year adjusted EBITDA was $170 million, while Industrial Services, including Phoenix Global, delivered $62.3 million.
  • Corporate and other expenses were $13.1 million. Capital expenditures were $66.8 million, slightly below guidance due to payment timing. SunCoke ended 2025 with $88.7 million cash and $132 million in revolver availability.

Q&A

  • Henry Hearle, B. Riley Securities, asked about the status of Algoma contract litigation. Gates responded, "We continue to pursue Algoma in -- it's in an arbitration. We're pursuing all legal means to recover our losses. So we absolutely believe we have an enforceable contract."
  • Hearle questioned Phoenix Global’s EBITDA contribution and integration synergies. Gates confirmed, "Yes, we are" still anticipating an annual EBITDA contribution of roughly $60 million and synergies of $5 million to $10 million in 2026 guidance.
  • Hearle also inquired about onetime Phoenix integration costs. Marinko replied, "Site closure costs of about $3.9 million... transaction costs of about $600,000 as well."
  • Nathan Martin, The Benchmark Company, asked about the permanence of the Haverhill I closure. Gates explained, "The Haverhill I could be restarted, but it would require a significant capital investment and it would take about 12 to 18 months to restart."
  • Martin asked about the impact of turbine failure and weather events. Gates said, "The impact from those events... that's going to aggregate to approximately a $10 million impact in the first quarter."
  • Martin requested details on industrial segment volume improvements. Gates stated that guidance includes a full year of the KRT contract and modest recovery at both KRT and CMT.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concern about ongoing litigation, integration costs, and operational disruptions, but questions remained fact-focused and constructive.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing contract enforcement, ongoing integration, and market recovery. Gates used phrases such as "we expect to prevail in our litigation" and "we are excited for the growth potential in this business."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remained cautious but less focused on immediate liquidity risk, while management’s confidence appeared more assured, supported by finalized contract extensions and integration progress.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 is more specific, with explicit EBITDA and cash flow targets, compared to the prior quarter’s tentative outlook.
  • The previous quarter centered on uncertainty from the Algoma breach and pending contract renewals; the current quarter confirms extensions for Granite City and Haverhill II.
  • Management’s tone shifted from cautious optimism to greater confidence, supported by operational and strategic developments.
  • Analyst focus moved from contract enforcement and dividend sustainability to operational recovery and integration execution.

Risks and Concerns

  • The breach of contract by Algoma remains a significant risk, with litigation ongoing and uncertainties regarding recovery of losses.
  • Integration of Phoenix Global involves onetime costs and ongoing IT and bonus expenses.
  • Operational risks include facility closures, turbine failures, and weather events, with the latter causing a $10 million impact in Q1 2026.
  • The company is exposed to market-driven terminal handling volumes, particularly at KRT and CMT.

Final Takeaway

SunCoke Energy concluded 2025 with stable core operations, highlighted by the successful integration of Phoenix Global, extension of key contracts, and continued capital return to shareholders. Despite challenges from the Algoma contract breach and operational disruptions, the company projects a meaningful recovery in 2026, with improved adjusted EBITDA, robust cash flow generation, and a focus on deleveraging and shareholder returns. Management underscores its commitment to executing on integration, maintaining operational excellence, and pursuing legal remedies to recover contract losses, positioning SunCoke for long-term growth and value creation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: CEVA outlines 8%-12% revenue growth target for 2026 while expanding AI and connectivity leadership
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CEVA, Inc. (CEVA) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Amir Panush highlighted that 2025 was "a landmark year for CEVA," emphasizing record quarterly revenue and a strategic breakthrough in AI and connectivity: "We strengthened our foundation, reinforced our leadership position in wireless connectivity and accelerated our expansion into AI for the Smart Edge." He stressed the company's unique position for "the Physical AI era" and noted a major NPU licensing win with a "leading PC OEM developing its next-generation AI personal compute architecture."
  • Panush underscored that "licensing revenue increased 11%, exceeding our expectations through strong execution across all 3 of our technologies pillars and reflecting broad demand across multiple end markets." He also cited 18 licensing agreements in the quarter, including "multiple Wi-Fi 7 and combo connectivity wins and a meaningful software engagement."
  • The CEO celebrated AI processor licensing representing "a meaningful portion of our licensing revenue in 2025," with the NPU deals carrying "higher per unit and longer-term royalty potential."
  • CFO Yaniv Arieli reported, "Revenue for the fourth quarter increased 7% year-over-year and 10% sequentially to an all-time record high of $31.3 million." He stated that "licensing and related revenue increased 11% year-over-year and 9% sequentially to $17.5 million," while "royalty revenue increased 2% year-over-year and 12% sequentially to $13.8 million."
  • Arieli added, "Non-GAAP operating margins and income were 18% of revenue and $5.7 million and grew 20% and 26% year-over-year, respectively."

Outlook

  • CEVA expects "total revenue...to grow 8% to 12% over 2025 with lower growth in the first half of the year and higher in the second half, similar to prior years and seasonal trends and subject to the memory pricing fluctuation and supply challenges." CFO Arieli stated, "we anticipate non-GAAP operating income and non-GAAP net income to increase significantly by approximately 35% to 40% year-over-year."
  • The company forecasts "total non-GAAP expenses in 2026 to be in the range of $104.4 million to $108.4 million," reflecting modest organic expense growth with FX impact.
  • For Q1 2026, "revenue is forecasted to be between $24 million to $28 million, sequentially lower than the record fourth quarter we just reported, but still significantly higher than the first quarter of 2025 at the midpoint."

Financial Results

  • CEVA posted "the highest quarterly revenue in CEVA's history" at $31.3 million for Q4 2025. Non-GAAP net income and diluted income per share for the quarter were $4.9 million and $0.18, compared to $2.7 million and $0.11 for Q4 2024.
  • The company shipped 606 million CEVA-powered devices in Q4, with record Wi-Fi shipments of 86 million units, up 30% year-over-year, and record cellular IoT shipments of 60 million units, also up 30% year-over-year.
  • Annual CEVA-powered device shipments reached 2.1 billion units in 2025, with Wi-Fi shipments growing 48% and cellular IoT units up 42% year-over-year. Bluetooth shipments were reported at 1.1 billion units for the year.
  • Cash and equivalents stood at approximately $222 million at year-end, boosted by a 3.5 million share offering for approximately $63 million net in Q4.

Q&A

  • Kevin Cassidy, Rosenblatt Securities: Asked about the NPU pipeline and end-market exposures. CEO Panush responded, "The pipeline overall keeps growing across pretty much all the different type of submarket segments that we see across the Smart Edge...very encouraged with how we have executed and how we see the future going to 2026."
  • Ruben Roy, Stifel: Inquired about competitive dynamics in the PC NPU win and royalty potential. Panush emphasized, "there is stronger and stronger need to really best-in-class performance...we are well, well positioned competitively, and that's why they picked us." CFO Arieli added about the $125 million lifetime royalty estimate, "6 of the NPU customers that have signed with us over the last year to 2 years should be ready in production by the end of the year and then probably or hopefully, royalty contribution in the beginning of 2027 for us."
  • Sujeeva De Silva, ROTH Capital: Sought clarity on the repeatability of the PC OEM win and Physical AI opportunities. Panush noted, "definitely, there is potential that other will follow suit with the same type of configuration" and identified robotics as a major Physical AI growth area.
  • Alek Valero, Loop Capital: Questioned 2026 guidance drivers and M&A. Arieli cited "stronger licensing," royalty ramp-ups, and currency impacts as key variables, while Panush added, "the goal...is to utilize that balance sheet to find additional assets out there in the IP domain that we can take on and expand even further our potential for growth and profitability."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed positive sentiment, offering congratulations and highlighting strong execution, but also probed for details on competitive differentiation, timing of royalty flows, and guidance sensitivity to memory supply and FX.
  • Management maintained a confident tone throughout, frequently referencing "encouraging momentum," "strong execution," and "exciting times," while providing detailed answers and reiterating their optimism for AI and connectivity growth.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained upbeat but was more specific about AI pipeline conversion, royalty potential, and the operational impact of FX headwinds. Analyst tone in both quarters was constructive but more focused this quarter on monetization timelines and strategic execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 revenue growth (8%-12%) is reaffirmed, with new detail on expected non-GAAP operating and net income growth (35%-40% year-over-year).
  • AI licensing momentum shifted from being a "very meaningful and growing part" of licensing revenue in Q3 to a "breakthrough" with a strategic PC OEM win and a $125 million estimated lifetime royalty pipeline in Q4.
  • Management's confidence in AI and Physical AI opportunities has grown, with increased specificity around royalty timing (beginning of 2027) and pipeline breadth across multiple end markets.
  • Expenses are expected to rise modestly, mainly due to FX impacts, with management emphasizing cost discipline and scalability.
  • Analyst focus shifted from pipeline sustainability and competitive differentiation in Q3 to more granular questions about royalty conversion, Physical AI, and capital deployment in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "memory pricing and supply constraints" as ongoing external risks impacting smartphone shipments and overall royalty timing.
  • FX headwinds are expected to increase non-U.S. dollar-based expenses, particularly for R&D in Europe and Israel, by around $5 million in 2026.
  • While a recovery from China-based handset customers was noted, management remains cautious about broader market conditions affecting royalty flows.
  • Analysts voiced concern about the timing and magnitude of AI royalty conversion, the competitive landscape in NPUs, and the ability to deploy new capital effectively for M&A.

Final Takeaway

CEVA closed 2025 with record revenue and growing momentum across AI and connectivity, marked by a breakthrough NPU licensing deal with a leading PC OEM and robust expansion into Physical AI applications. Management projects 8%-12% revenue growth in 2026 and anticipates significant gains in non-GAAP operating and net income, underpinned by a diversified customer base and disciplined cost management. With an expanding royalty pipeline and a strengthened balance sheet, CEVA positions itself for continued leadership in Smart Edge and AI-driven markets, despite near-term headwinds from memory supply and FX volatility.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: FedEx execs: Most challenging trade situation in years, but situation has stabilized
data.description:
Executives at FedEx (FDX) described the current trade situation as the most “challenging” in recent years but said that they don't see any further major changes on the horizon. Meanwhile, leaders at the package delivery giant commented on some broad changes being driven by the trade environment, including a shift in supply-chain philosophy at many companies.

"This has been a most challenging set of circumstances that I have encountered in recent past in terms of the changing dynamics of trade," CEO Rajesh Subramaniam stated at the company's analyst day last week. 

Still, the firm's CFO, John Dietrich, expressed hope that the situation has reached an equilibrium for now. "[Our] outlook does not anticipate any further changes, material changes to the global trade policy environment," he said.

Commenting on the overall situation for supply chains, Richard Smith, COO of FDX's Airline and International unit, stated: "Manufacturers are diversifying supply chains from single-country models to distributed networks as near and fringe shoring reshapes global trade flows."

data.title: Helus gains on peer-reviewed data for psychedelic therapy
data.description:
Helus Pharma (HELP) added ~11% on Tuesday after Nature Medicine published data from a mid-stage trial for SPL026, an intravenously delivered psychedelic compound developed by the Canadian pharmaceutical company.

The Phase 2a trial, designed to evaluate the serotonergic hallucinogen in moderate-to-severe major depressive disorder, reached its primary endpoint, as there was a statistically significant and clinically meaningful decline in depression symptoms over two weeks.

The findings were based on a clinical measure called the Montgomery-Åsberg Depression Rating Scale (“MADRS”), which, in reaction to SPL026, indicated a 7.35 difference over two weeks compared to placebo.

“This publication represents an important validation of short-acting serotonergic agonists as a clinically meaningful approach in mental health treatments,” CEO Michael Cola said.

While Helus (HELP) is no longer developing intravenous SPL026, it is running a mid-stage trial for a novel serotonergic agonist called HLP004 in patients with generalized anxiety disorder. Topline data from the study are expected in Q1 2026.

data.title: Wall Street meets the ballot box: Roundhill Investments files for binary election ETFs
data.description:
Roundhill Investments has filed a novel lineup of politically linked exchange traded funds with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission, offering investors targeted exposure to the outcomes of key U.S. elections. 

The proposed suite includes six funds—covering Democratic and Republican victories in the presidency, Senate, and House—each built on the same structural framework but calibrated to a specific electoral scenario. The products aim to deliver capital appreciation only if the designated political outcome occurs. 

At the core of each ETF is the use of binary “event contracts,” derivative instruments that pay a fixed amount if a specified political event happens and zero if it does not. Roundhill plans to gain most of this exposure through swap agreements referencing the contracts, while maintaining flexibility to hold them directly. Each fund intends to allocate at least 80% of net assets to derivatives tied to its respective election outcome.

The filings emphasize the high-risk nature of the strategy. Because the funds’ payoffs hinge entirely on a single political result, they could lose nearly all their value if the targeted party fails to prevail. 

Proposed funds include: 

  • Roundhill Democratic President ETF (BLUP) 
  • Roundhill Republican President ETF (REDP)
  • Roundhill Democratic Senate ETF (BLUS) 
  • Roundhill Republican Senate ETF (REDS)
  • Roundhill Democratic House ETF (BLUH) 
  • Roundhill Republican House ETF (REDH) 

If approved, the lineup would represent one of the most explicit attempts yet to package election outcomes into ETF form for public investors.

Other Political ETFs: (YALL), (ACVF), (MAGA), (DEMZ), and (NANC).

S&P 500 ETFs: (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (FXAIX), (VFIAX), (VFFSX), and (SWPPX).
